segments.text: Appreciate your time this afternoon.
We have half an hour sit down,
welcome some introductory comments
and you know, questions from members of the committee.
Brilliant. Thank you, Mr. Chair.
And I would like to say some introductory comments, just,
um, to recognize the hard worker customs.
Very happy to discuss customs work and protect new and,
and protecting New Zealand and to support the efficient
and safe flow of trade and travelers.
Um, but a few comments that I'd like to specifically bring
to your attention targeting transnational organized crime
remains a top priority for me, both as minister of customs
and as associate minister of Police.
Unfortunately, as you'll be aware, this is an area
of growing concern, but customs and its network of domestic
and international partner agencies,
especially New Zealand police are doing a great job.
As one example, earlier this year in March, 2024, customs
and Police helped United States law enforcement prevent
almost 200 kilograms
of methamphetamine arriving in New Zealand and Australia.
This helped dismantle a US based criminal network.
So far this year, customs overseas liaison
and partner networks have stopped more than two tons
of drugs before it reached our border.
In addition, supported through intelligence
and targeting customs,
frontline offices seized more than three tons
of illegal drugs at our border.
Customs overseas liaison offices across the Pacific Asia
and the Atlantic play a valuable
and strategic role in this collaborative work.
These offices are also key support
for New Zealand's trade interests.
As an example of that support, a few months ago in July,
customs liaison officer in Beijing helped address delays in
to 8.5 million worth of exports held up at Tja,
um, port, which included time critical perishable goods.
This is a very small part of the trade customs facilitates,
and of course it also collects significant revenue,
17.4 billion last year.
To conclude, I would really sincerely want
to acknowledge custom staff and its leadership.
The organization has a strong reputation both in New Zealand
and overseas as a highly professional agency that operates
with integrity, agility, and diligence every day.
Customs do a critical work, custom staff do critical work,
and they do it to a very high standard,
and I thank them for that.
And with that, I'm happy to take questions.
Excellent. Thank you very much honor.
Thank you, minister. It's good to see you.
And, um, like you, I'd like to commend customs
and all of it staff members
because you're right, they do do a really brilliant job
of protecting, um, our board.
My question is about revenue though, um, minister,
because as you know, customs is the organization
that collects, I think,
the most revenue, um, for the country.
So we've had a revenue decline
of 1.2 billion this year collected by customs compared
to the last fiscal year.
And I just wanted to know what specific factors contributed
to the 6% decrease in the Crown revenue collected
by customs in 20 23, 24 compared
to the previous financial year, especially in light of, I
I remember one of, um, the stated goals
that you had from a previous session was
that you wanted revenue growth, um, through increased trade.
Yeah. And that, that is a, a big component.
We have a clear, I mean, the two priorities I set
for customers was to support the government's priority
of doubling our exports over the next 10 years.
And then the other component is
around the organized crime statistics.
So revenue growth is important,
but of course, revenue growth is impacted by our economy
and, and the, the state of our trading.
But in terms of specific
and around areas where we've seen that, um, reduction,
I think of turn to the comptroller
and any specific information.
So, uh, it's, it's fairly straightforward.
There have been fewer imports, export,
and we collect on those.
And when those are down,
there is less revenue for the Crown.
And we are the second largest collector of revenue
after in the revenue.
I do want to know, though, just through the details.
So was it in general
because of, of reduced, uh, trade global economic conditions
or domestic policy changes?
What areas, um, specifically did you see this reduction?
It's pretty much across the board, so I wouldn't say it,
you know, the, the New Zealand economy has
been in recession.
I think that's pretty, pretty well understood.
Uh, we have been, I importing this,
we've been exporting this.
If I can ask another, uh, specific question.
Was the 1.2 billion, uh,
revenue short form more pronounced in specific sectors
as you know, as you could see, was it fuel,
alcohol, tobacco?
What, what were those? Or was it just everything? It was
Less a higher value goods imported,
and it's a gst so it's a smaller take of gst,
But this was forecast by treasury,
so it is in my treasury forecast.
So do you have any, um,
any strategies for more efficient collection of revenue?
Would that actually assist in collecting more
of this revenue or No, only
because so few of this, these goods not coming through.
Yeah, I think, I think it is, um, genuinely around, um,
you know, definitely a reduction in the high value goods.
And I think that's across machinery
and equipment, big, big ticket items
that haven't been being bought in.
But the other component I think is recognizing
that there is a lot of work that customs has done to build
and developed, um,
the follow up round trade agreements that have been established.
So there has been significant work in that space, um,
to facilitate those trade agreements getting off the ground.
And I think that's one of the things I really wanted
to recognize around the stuff that's sort of unseen
by the public in terms of what customs does in terms
of facilitating and operationalizing those
agreements as they come through.
So I'll ask a different question.
How does New Zealand's decline in customs revenue compared
to other regions or other global countries?
Or are we unique in this decline
or do we actually compare to other OECD countries?
Is every other country declining in terms
of the revenue that they're collecting? Do
You? To be honest, I haven't
looked at that.
We, we worry very much about what we are collecting
and we, uh, treasury forecast is
what we judge ourselves against.
But I imagine if you look around the world,
it'll be a mixed picture
and, uh, some economies in a number
of economies globally have had, um, slow code, not, not
so much the US probably,
but if it helps China, it's a much slower economy.
Perhaps if I could add state
that commentary on Could you Yeah, sorry.
Just come forward to so
that we can hear it through microphones.
Um, treasury would consider the impact of changes to our
p volumes and values, um, in comparison
with other countries, and that would include
that commentary when they do the, uh, economic forecast.
So it's a, it's an answer, a, a question
that could be answered by treasury officials.
Um, we would provide advice to them on what we are seeing.
We are seeing, as we have said, uh, changes
and the volumes of, um, high value imports
and that's impacting our GST tank.
But treasury would do the analysis
of whether there are broader economic factors,
including whether other countries slowdowns
or import changes are, uh, different to what we are seeing.

while there's been a decline in the sort of trade volumes,

a big increase in the, uh, air travel

Basically, but actually, uh,
air travel still hasn't fully recovered
Right. From the pre pandemic levels.
So we still running around 93% of the 2019 figures.
Uh, cruise volumes came back strongly last year,

of volumes again next year.

Thank you very much.

for cocaine are about five kilos a week in end,

see 3.7 kilos, about 3.7 tons of drugs this year,

greater use probably is whether the gangs are trying

It's probably happening too,
but it is really difficult to
to know how much we we're missing.

far, far more than is being used.
But of course we are missing some.
I think something else that's worth mentioning is seizures
now are often very large.
So if you look back 20 years, 20 years ago,
we seized one kilo
of methamphetamines in the whole year, one kilo.
Now I get stats every morning on my desk,
what we seized the day before.
And we, if we're not seizing between 10

that's how the market has changed.
And, uh, you may have seen in the media recently operation
what was nearly 500 kis of less in one shipment that we

good cooperation lease.
But if you miss one 500 kilo shipment,
it's not like missing a 20 kilo shipment.
So we are working incredibly hard, keep up with this.
I'd also say this is a global trend.
Again, this is what we've see all around the world.

Just, just tos on.

Thursday 5 December 2024 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee

Item of business: 

2023/24 Annual review of the New Zealand Customs Service
